questions.text: What is the size of Parvovirus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parvoviruses are non-enveloped, icosahedral particles 18 to 26 nm in diameter. Plus and minus DNA strands are packaged into separate virions in approximately equal proportion.
Parvoviruses are non-enveloped, icosahedral particles 18 to 26 nm in diameter. Plus and minus DNA strands are packaged into separate virions in approximately equal proportion.

questions.text: Eukaryotic, multicellular, and consumers belong to _ kingdom.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Kingdom Monera includes unicellular prokaryotes.
Kingdom Protista includes organisms that are both unicellular as well as multicellular.
Fungi are multicellular, eukaryotic but they are not consumers but rather have an absorptive mode of nutrition.
Kingdom Animalia includes organisms that are eukaryotic, multicellular, and consumers.

questions.explanation: Kingdom Monera: It comprises unicellular, prokaryotic organisms, which can be heterotrophic or autotrophic, having no well-defined nucleus and no organelles. Kingdom Protista: Protists are eukaryotic, unicellular organisms They can be autotrophs or heterotrophs. Kingdom Fungi: Fungi can be single-celled or very complex multicellular organisms. All fungi are heterotrophic, which means that they get the energy they need to live from other organisms. They are eukaryotes. Kingdom Animalia: They are eukaryotic, multicellular, and heterotrophic.

questions.text: The numbers of capsomeres found in adenovirus capsid is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Herpes virus has 162 capsomeres.
This option is not correct for an adenovirus.

The adenovirus particle consists of an icosahedral protein shell surrounding a protein core that contains the linear, double-stranded DNA genome. The shell, which is 70 to 100 nm in diameter, is made up of 252 structural capsomeres.
This option is not correct for an adenovirus.

questions.explanation: The adenovirus particle consists of an icosahedral protein shell surrounding a protein core that contains the linear, double-stranded DNA genome. The shell, which is 70 to 100 nm in diameter, is made up of 252 structural capsomeres. 162 capsomeres are found in herpes virus. So, option C is correct.

questions.text: Now-a-days every newborn gets regular shots of vaccine for polio. It contains _ for polio to make a child immune against this disease.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: A vaccine elicits an immune response by the host so that the host is better equipped to fight against the invading organism, in any potential future instance, via the production of memory cells. It may do so by containing the antigens of the invading organism (in this case, poliovirus) so that the host’s immune system produces memory cells against the specific organism, thereby conferring active immunity against the disease. Antibiotics (option A) are not present in vaccines; they are drugs used to treat bacterial illnesses. Antibodies (option B) are not present in vaccines; they are proteins, produced by plasma cells, to ward off infection. They are produced by the immune system to fight an active infection, but they are not used in vaccines since they do not confer active immunity as the host does not produce memory cells against the invading organism. Antisera (option D) is a blood serum preparation that contains antibodies against a certain antigen or disease; they are used as a treatment for certain conditions and do not confer active immunity to the individual.

questions.text: When were bacteriophages discovered by Twort?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacteriophages were discovered independently by Frederick W. Twort in Great Britain (1915) and Félix d'Hérelle in France (1917).
Bacteriophages were discovered independently by Frederick W. Twort in Great Britain (1915) and Félix d'Hérelle in France (1917).

questions.text: HIV is a:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: HIV (human immunodeficiency virus) is a virus that attacks the body's immune system. If HIV is not treated, it can lead to AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome). There is currently no effective cure. Once people get HIV, they have it for life.

questions.text: Which of the following diseases is sexually transmitted?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) is a chronic, potentially life-threatening condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). By damaging your immune system, HIV interferes with your body's ability to fight infection and disease. You can get HIV from contact with infected blood, semen, or vaginal fluids. Most people get the virus by having unprotected sex with someone who has HIV. Another common way of getting it is by sharing drug needles with someone who is infected with HIV. Hence, Option B is correct. Tuberculosis is caused by bacteria that spread from person to person through microscopic droplets released into the air. This can happen when someone with the untreated, active form of tuberculosis coughs, speaks, sneezes, spits, laughs, or sings. Hence, Option A is incorrect. When a mosquito bites a person infected with a dengue virus, the virus enters the mosquito. Then, when the infected mosquito bites another person, the virus enters that person's bloodstream and causes an infection. Hence, Option C is incorrect. A person can get cholera by drinking water or eating food contaminated with cholera bacteria. In an epidemic, the source of the contamination is usually the feces of an infected person that contaminates water or food. Hence, Option D is incorrect.

questions.text: What type of virus is the smallpox virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Like other OPXVs, VARV and MPXV produce large brick-shaped particles that are approximately 140–260 nm in diameter and 220–450 nm long, containing a linear DNA genome approximately 200 kb in length. The genome, enclosed within an enveloped capsid, is monopartite and encodes around 200 genes.

Examples of enveloped viruses include: influenza, human cytomegalovirus (HCMV), HIV, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), vaccinia virus, and human coronaviruses (such as NL63, 229E, OC43, and SARS-CoV-2).
Examples of DNA viruses are herpes, smallpox, hepatitis B, adenoviruses, and warts.
Some examples of non-enveloped viruses are norovirus, enterovirus, adenovirus, and rhinovirus.

questions.explanation: Like other OPXVs, VARV and MPXV produce large brick-shaped particles that are approximately 140–260 nm in diameter and 220–450 nm long, containing a linear DNA genome approximately 200 kb in length. The genome, enclosed within an enveloped capsid, is monopartite and encodes around 200 genes.

questions.text: Which was the first virus to be successfully crystalized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tobacco mosaic virus was the first virus to be crystallized. It was achieved by Wendell Meredith Stanley in 1935.

questions.explanation: Tobacco mosaic virus was the first virus to be crystallized. It was achieved by Wendell Meredith Stanley in 1935.

questions.text: Binomial nomenclature was proposed for the first time by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is incorrect because Charles Darwin proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection.
It is incorrect because Rudolph Virchow rejected the idea of abiogenesis.
It is incorrect because Louis Pasteur made different contributions in the field of biology, such as the development of vaccines and pasteurisation of milk.
The first system of naming species on the basis of their classification was proposed by Carolus Linneaus called binomial nomenclature in 1707 hence it is correct.
It is incorrect because Robert Brown discovered the nucleus in a cell.

questions.explanation: Tyrannosaurus rex is probably the most widely known binomial. The formal introduction of this system of naming species is credited to Carl Linnaeus, effectively beginning with his work Species Plantarum in 1753.

questions.text: The shape of the tobacco mosaic virus is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect as TMV is not spring shape.
This is factual recall from the chapter of acellular life which states that TMV is a helical virus that appears rod shaped so option B is most suitable.
Option C is incorrect as TMV is not comma shape.
Option D is incorrect as TMV is not spherical shape.

questions.explanation: This is factual recall from the chapter of acellular life which states that TMV is a helical virus that appears rod shaped so option B is most suitable.

questions.text: Chicken pox is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis A is an inflammation of the liver caused by the hepatitis A virus (HAV). The virus is primarily spread when an uninfected (and unvaccinated) person ingests food or water that is contaminated with the feces of an infected person.
Chickenpox is a highly contagious disease caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV). It can cause an itchy, blister-like rash among other symptoms.
The flu is caused by an influenza virus. Most people get the flu when they breathe in tiny airborne droplets from the coughs or sneezes of someone who has the flu.
HIV (human immunodeficiency virus) is a virus that attacks the body's immune system. If HIV is not treated, it can lead to AIDS.

Rabies is a preventable viral disease most often transmitted through the bite of a rabid animal. The rabies virus infects the central nervous system of mammals, ultimately causing disease in the brain and death.

questions.explanation: Chickenpox is an infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus. It causes an itchy rash with small, fluid-filled blisters. Chickenpox is highly contagious to people who haven't had the disease or been vaccinated against it.

questions.text: The viruses are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cellular: Viruses are not considered cellular organisms because they lack the essential characteristics of living cells. They cannot carry out metabolic processes or reproduce on their own. Instead, they rely on host cells to replicate and perform their life cycle.

Prokaryotes: Prokaryotes are single-celled organisms that lack a nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles. Viruses are distinct from prokaryotes because they are not cells at all. They are much simpler in structure, consisting of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat called a capsid.

Option C is the correct answer. Viruses do not have cells. They have a protein coat that protects their genetic material (either DNA or RNA). But they do not have a cell membrane or other organelles (for example, ribosomes or mitochondria) that cells have

Eukaryotes: Eukaryotes are organisms whose cells have a nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles. Viruses differ from eukaryotes because they lack cellular structures and are unable to carry out the functions associated with eukaryotic cells.

Visible with naked eye: Viruses are incredibly small and cannot be seen with the naked eye. They typically range in size from about 20 to 300 nanometers, which is far below the threshold of human visual perception. Specialized instruments such as electron microscopes are required to observe viruses.

questions.explanation: Viruses are non-cellular. They only have a genome covered by a protein coat called a capsid.

questions.text: Closely related species are grouped together into _ .
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A family represents a group of closely related genera.
Class is a taxonomic group consisting of one or more related orders.
It is a closely related group of phylum.
Closely related kingdoms are called phyla.

Closely related species resembling each other are grouped together into a Genus. The hierarchy of classification is Species < Genus < Family < Order< Class < Phylum/Division < Kingdom.The genus is the group which consists of the organisms which have related characters.

questions.explanation: Closely related species are grouped together into a “Genus”.

questions.text: Which of the following is the simplest form of pathogens causing diseases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are not the simplest form of pathogens causing diseases because they require a host cell to replicate and cause disease, whereas bacteria are single-celled organisms that can cause disease on their own.

Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens, which include bacteria, fungi, protozoa, worms, viruses, and even infectious proteins called prions. The most simple and smallest type of pathogen is prion

Fungi are not the simplest form of pathogens causing diseases because they are more complex organisms than viruses and bacteria, have a defined cell structure, and often require more elaborate environments to thrive and cause infections.
Amoeba may be simple in structure but they possess various virulence factors and mechanisms that allow them to cause diseases, making them more complex than other simple pathogens.

questions.explanation: Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens, which include bacteria, fungi, protozoa, worms, viruses, and even infectious proteins called prions. The most simple and the smallest type of pathogens are prions.

questions.text: Mark the one Kingdom having the most conspicuous living organisms?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monera includes single-celled organisms like bacteria and archaea, which are small and often not easily noticeable.
Protista includes single-celled organisms like algae and amoeba, which can be larger than bacteria but still not as easily noticeable as multicellular organisms.
Kingdom Animalia has the most conspicuous subsets of organisms because theoretically they have evolved the most and are the most advanced version of evolved species.
Plants are not conspicuous as animals because they don't move and their visibility is often limited.

questions.explanation: Kingdom Animalia has the most conspicous subsets of organims because theoretically they have evolved the most and are the most advanced version of evolved species.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a viral disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AIDS is a viral infection and is caused by HIV.
Malaria is not a viral disease and is caused by protozoa plasmodium.
Influenza is also a viral disease caused by the influenza virus.
Chickenpox is also a viral disease and is caused by the Varicella-zoster virus (VZV).
Rabies is also a viral disease and is caused by rabies lyssavirus.

questions.explanation: Malaria is not a viral disease and is caused by protozoa plasmodium. All other options are viral infections that include: AIDS caused by HIV virus, influenza caused by the influenza virus, chickenpox is caused by the Varicella-zoster virus(VZV) and rabies is caused by rabies lyssavirus.

questions.text: Radiations are classified by its _ nature.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Radiations are mostly ionizing in nature. They are classified according to their ionizing strength.Radiations are classified by their electromagnetic or particle nature.
They are nit classified according to their non ionizing strength.
They are classified according to their ionizing strength.
They are classified according to their ionizing strength.

questions.text: At which stage of HIV infection does one usually show symptoms of AIDS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Within 15 days of sexual contact with an infected person
options.explanation: Factually incorrect.

options.text: When the infected retro virus enters host cells
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because HIV attacks the immune system, specifically targeting the CD4 cells (T cells). HIV infection progresses through several stages by infecting Helper T cells.

options.text: When HIV damages a large number of helper T-lymphocytes
options.explanation: This option is correct because HIV attacks the immune system, specifically targeting the CD4 cells (T cells). HIV infection progresses through several stages by infecting Helper T cells.

options.text: When the viral DNA is produced by reverse transcriptase
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because HIV attacks the immune system, specifically targeting the CD4 cells (T cells). HIV infection progresses through several stages by infecting Helper T cells.

questions.explanation: AIDS is a disorder of the cell-mediated immune system of the body. The virus responsible for AIDS is HIV(Human immunodeficiency virus). There is a reduction in the number of helper T cells which stimulate antibody production by B-cells. This results in the loss of natural defense against viral infection. HIV attacks the immune system, specifically targeting the CD4 cells (T cells). HIV infection progresses through several stages by infecting Helper T cells.
